summary:
The Mature Harappan phase of the Indus Valley Civilization, dating from approximately 2600 to 1900 BCE, represents the peak development of one of the ancient world's largest and most sophisticated urban societies. Stretching across an area larger than either contemporary Egypt or Mesopotamia — encompassing present-day Pakistan and northwestern India — this civilization developed in relative isolation from the other great river valley cultures of the Bronze Age, yet demonstrated levels of urban planning, sanitation infrastructure, and standardization that were not matched in many parts of the world for centuries.

The two largest known cities of the Mature Harappan period, Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, each housed populations estimated between 20,000 and 50,000 people. Both exhibit a remarkably consistent urban layout: a citadel mound to the west with large public buildings, and a lower city to the east laid out on an approximate grid of streets. Standardized fired-brick construction, using bricks of consistent proportions across sites separated by hundreds of kilometers, reflects a degree of cultural and possibly administrative integration that continues to astonish researchers. The Great Bath at Mohenjo-daro — a large watertight tank lined with bitumen sealant and accessed by staircases — may have served ritual purification functions and remains one of the earliest examples of sophisticated water management infrastructure.

The Harappan sanitation system was the most advanced of the ancient world. Nearly every major dwelling in Mohenjo-daro was connected to a centralized drainage network of covered brick-lined channels running beneath the streets, allowing wastewater to flow out of the city. This infrastructure implies not only significant engineering knowledge but also a degree of civic organization — someone had to plan, build, and maintain these systems — though the nature of Harappan governance remains deeply uncertain, as no palace complexes, royal tombs, or unambiguous elite architecture comparable to Mesopotamian or Egyptian examples have been identified.

Harappan trade networks extended across an enormous area. Lapis lazuli arrived from mines in modern Afghanistan; carnelian and chalcedony beads were produced at workshops in Gujarat and exported widely; cotton textiles — among the earliest in the world — were produced and possibly traded. Contact with the [[event:45|Akkadian Empire]] is attested by Harappan-style seals found in Mesopotamian contexts and references in cuneiform texts to a trading partner called Meluhha, which scholars generally identify with the Indus region. Gulf traders at sites like Bahrain served as intermediaries in this maritime commerce.

Perhaps the most enduring mystery of the Indus Valley Civilization is its undeciphered script. Thousands of inscribed soapstone seals, copper tablets, and pottery sherds bear short sequences of signs — typically five to ten symbols — that have resisted all decipherment attempts despite extensive scholarly effort. Without the ability to read these texts, fundamental questions about Harappan religion, governance, language family, and social organization cannot be definitively answered. The iconography on the seals does reveal a rich symbolic world including a horned figure in a yogic seated posture, animals such as the unicorn, bull, and rhinoceros, and composite mythological creatures.

The Mature Harappan period gave way to a post-urban phase around 1900 BCE, with the largest cities gradually declining in population and material sophistication over the following centuries. The causes remain debated: shifts in the monsoon pattern leading to reduced river flow, the drying or course-changing of the Ghaggar-Hakra river system (which many scholars identify with the ancient Sarasvati River), tectonic activity, and possible disruption of trade networks have all been proposed. Population did not simply vanish but appears to have dispersed eastward into the Gangetic plain, where elements of Harappan material culture and possibly religious traditions contributed to the cultural substrate from which [[event:33|Early Harappan]] communities had grown, and which eventually fed into the later developments of South Asian civilization. The Mature Harappan phase is recognized today as one of the great achievements of the ancient world, even as its full history remains partially shrouded in the silence of an unread script.
